case_name: Surendra Banjara vs State Of U.P. & Another
text: 20. In Dashrath Rupsingh Rathod 's case (supra), it was also also contemplated as to what will hapepen of the pending cases. In the case at hand, the position being very clear that it was very initial stage of the case as only summoning order was rejected by the Magistrate's court, which order has been reversed by the Revisional Court, therefore, stage of Section 145 (2) of the N.I. Act was yet to reach. Therefore, in this case the proper course would be for the courts below to return the complaint to the presenter of the same to be presented before the court having jurisdiction, but this course has not been adopted by the courts below which is found erroneous. 21. The next point which this court would like to take up is whether the case was hit by proviso to Section 138 (b) N.I. Act. For the sake of convenience, the said Section is reproduced hereinbelow: "138(b)"the payee or the hlder in due course of the cheque, as the case may be, makes a demand for the payment of the said amount of money by giving a notice in writing, to the drawer of the cheque, (within thirty days) of the receipt of information by him from the bank regarding the return of the cheque as unpaid; and" 22. It is apparent from the above provision that the period for issuing notice by payee or holder in due course is thirty days from the date of receipt of information by him from the bank regarding return of the cheque as unpaid. The question is, therefore, that in the case at hand, when the information was received by the payee/holder in due course opposite party no. 2- complainant. It is alleged by him in counter affidavit that though the cheques were dishonoured on 23.8.2014 as was mentioned in the memos of information but the same was not communicated to him till 20.9.2014. Therefore, according to him he could receive information about dishonour of cheques only on 20.9.2014 and from there onwards thirty days have to be counted within which the notice could have issued to the revisionist. In the case at hand, the notice has been issued on 1.10.2014, therefore, the said notice would not entail any infirmity and would be held to have been issued within thirty days.

case_name: Surendra Banjara vs State Of U.P. & Another
text: "138(b)"the payee or the hlder in due course of the cheque, as the case may be, makes a demand for the payment of the said amount of money by giving a notice in writing, to the drawer of the cheque, (within thirty days) of the receipt of information by him from the bank regarding the return of the cheque as unpaid; and" 22. It is apparent from the above provision that the period for issuing notice by payee or holder in due course is thirty days from the date of receipt of information by him from the bank regarding return of the cheque as unpaid. The question is, therefore, that in the case at hand, when the information was received by the payee/holder in due course opposite party no. 2- complainant. It is alleged by him in counter affidavit that though the cheques were dishonoured on 23.8.2014 as was mentioned in the memos of information but the same was not communicated to him till 20.9.2014. Therefore, according to him he could receive information about dishonour of cheques only on 20.9.2014 and from there onwards thirty days have to be counted within which the notice could have issued to the revisionist. In the case at hand, the notice has been issued on 1.10.2014, therefore, the said notice would not entail any infirmity and would be held to have been issued within thirty days. 23. In this regard, the reliance is placed by the learned counsel for the opposite party no. 2- complainant on Kamlesh Kumar V. State of Bihar and another , 2014 (2) SCC 424. Paragraph 13 of the said judgment is relevant, which is reporduced hereinbelow:- "13. The crucial question is as to on which date the complainant received the information about the dishonour of the cheque? As per the appellant, the respondent complainant received the information about the dishonour of the cheque on 10.11.2008. However, the respondent complainant has disputed the same. However, we would like to add that at the time of arguments the aforesaid submission of the appellant was not refuted. After the judgment was reserved, the complainant has filed the affidavit alleging therein that he received the bank memo of the bouncing of cheque on 17.11.2008 and therefore, legal notice sent on 17.12.2008 is within the period 30 days from the date of information."

case_name: Surendra Banjara vs State Of U.P. & Another
text: 23. In this regard, the reliance is placed by the learned counsel for the opposite party no. 2- complainant on Kamlesh Kumar V. State of Bihar and another , 2014 (2) SCC 424. Paragraph 13 of the said judgment is relevant, which is reporduced hereinbelow:- "13. The crucial question is as to on which date the complainant received the information about the dishonour of the cheque? As per the appellant, the respondent complainant received the information about the dishonour of the cheque on 10.11.2008. However, the respondent complainant has disputed the same. However, we would like to add that at the time of arguments the aforesaid submission of the appellant was not refuted. After the judgment was reserved, the complainant has filed the affidavit alleging therein that he received the bank memo of the bouncing of cheque on 17.11.2008 and therefore, legal notice sent on 17.12.2008 is within the period 30 days from the date of information." 24. It is apparent from the above that the court had held that normally it would have called upon the parties to prove their respective versions before the trial court for leading their evidence on the point as to when the cheques were dishonoured, information was received but in the above case , it was not required due to position of fact being clear on record. But in the case at hand, the opposite party no. 2- complainant had clearly stated on oath that information could be received by him about the cheque having been dishonoured/bounced on 20.9.2014. In case there was any controversy with regard to the said fact, the parties could have led evidence on this point before the court below during the trial. Therefore, the finding in this regard by the learned revisional court below cannot be held to be wrong that since the opposite party no. 2- complainant had stated on oath about the knowledge of cheques having been bounced to have occurred to him on 20.9.2014, the same could not have been dis-believed by the Magistrate's court outright to reject the complaint. This court's view is in consonance with the view expressed by learned revisional court below and finds that this was a matter which could have been decided only after taking evidence from both the sides during trial.

case_name: Surendra Banjara vs State Of U.P. & Another
text: "13. The crucial question is as to on which date the complainant received the information about the dishonour of the cheque? As per the appellant, the respondent complainant received the information about the dishonour of the cheque on 10.11.2008. However, the respondent complainant has disputed the same. However, we would like to add that at the time of arguments the aforesaid submission of the appellant was not refuted. After the judgment was reserved, the complainant has filed the affidavit alleging therein that he received the bank memo of the bouncing of cheque on 17.11.2008 and therefore, legal notice sent on 17.12.2008 is within the period 30 days from the date of information." 24. It is apparent from the above that the court had held that normally it would have called upon the parties to prove their respective versions before the trial court for leading their evidence on the point as to when the cheques were dishonoured, information was received but in the above case , it was not required due to position of fact being clear on record. But in the case at hand, the opposite party no. 2- complainant had clearly stated on oath that information could be received by him about the cheque having been dishonoured/bounced on 20.9.2014. In case there was any controversy with regard to the said fact, the parties could have led evidence on this point before the court below during the trial. Therefore, the finding in this regard by the learned revisional court below cannot be held to be wrong that since the opposite party no. 2- complainant had stated on oath about the knowledge of cheques having been bounced to have occurred to him on 20.9.2014, the same could not have been dis-believed by the Magistrate's court outright to reject the complaint. This court's view is in consonance with the view expressed by learned revisional court below and finds that this was a matter which could have been decided only after taking evidence from both the sides during trial. 25. Lastly argument in passing was made by learned counsel for the revisionist- accused that when the cheque itself had a limit of Rs. 50,000/- how could it be believed that cheques of an amount higher to that value could have been issued by the revisionist- accused in favour of opposite party no. 2.

case_name: Surendra Banjara vs State Of U.P. & Another
text: 24. It is apparent from the above that the court had held that normally it would have called upon the parties to prove their respective versions before the trial court for leading their evidence on the point as to when the cheques were dishonoured, information was received but in the above case , it was not required due to position of fact being clear on record. But in the case at hand, the opposite party no. 2- complainant had clearly stated on oath that information could be received by him about the cheque having been dishonoured/bounced on 20.9.2014. In case there was any controversy with regard to the said fact, the parties could have led evidence on this point before the court below during the trial. Therefore, the finding in this regard by the learned revisional court below cannot be held to be wrong that since the opposite party no. 2- complainant had stated on oath about the knowledge of cheques having been bounced to have occurred to him on 20.9.2014, the same could not have been dis-believed by the Magistrate's court outright to reject the complaint. This court's view is in consonance with the view expressed by learned revisional court below and finds that this was a matter which could have been decided only after taking evidence from both the sides during trial. 25. Lastly argument in passing was made by learned counsel for the revisionist- accused that when the cheque itself had a limit of Rs. 50,000/- how could it be believed that cheques of an amount higher to that value could have been issued by the revisionist- accused in favour of opposite party no. 2. 26. It is a matter to be decided by court undertaking trial of this case to give finding as to whether the amount which is within the cheque limit i.e. Rs. 50,000/- only could be released or the amount higher in denomination to Rs. 50,000/- could also be payable. Therefore, this point needs to be considered by the trial Court.

case_name: Surendra Banjara vs State Of U.P. & Another
text: 25. Lastly argument in passing was made by learned counsel for the revisionist- accused that when the cheque itself had a limit of Rs. 50,000/- how could it be believed that cheques of an amount higher to that value could have been issued by the revisionist- accused in favour of opposite party no. 2. 26. It is a matter to be decided by court undertaking trial of this case to give finding as to whether the amount which is within the cheque limit i.e. Rs. 50,000/- only could be released or the amount higher in denomination to Rs. 50,000/- could also be payable. Therefore, this point needs to be considered by the trial Court. 27. In view of above, the judgment and order dated 16.10.2015 of the revisional court is upheld as regards maintainability of the complaint and for remand of the matter to the court below for proceeding with the complaint in accordance with law. To that extent this revision is rejected. However, the trial court is directed to proceed to decide this case in accordance with the direction given in the body of this judgment as regards jurisdiction of the trial court. Order Date :- 12.3.2018 A.P. Pandey

case_name: Saurabh Kumar Banerjee vs . Pooja Agarwal
text: Delhi District Court Saurabh Kumar Banerjee vs . Pooja Agarwal on 27 March, 2021 1 IN THE COURT OF MS. SWATI GUPTA, METROPOLITAN MAGISTRATE (SOUTH) 04 N.I. ACT, SAKET COURTS: NEW DELHI CT Cases 5633/2017 SAURABH KUMAR BANERJEE Vs. POOJA AGARWAL 1. Complaint Case number : 5633 2. Name of the complainant : Saurabh Kumar Banerjee S/o Sh. Sanat Kumar R/o T-25/1, Malviya Nagar New Delhi. 3. Name and address of the accused : Pooja Aggarwal D/o Sh. Sunil Aggarwal R/o A-52, DLF Regal Garden, Sec-90, Gurgaon. 4. Offence complained of or proved : Under Section 138 of the Negotiable Instruments Act, 1881. 5. Plea of the accused : Pleaded not guilty 6. Final Order : Acquittal 7. Date of institution : 11.05.2017 8. Date on which reserved for judgement : 10.03.2021 9. Date of judgement : 27.03.2021 Digitally signed SWATI by SWATI GUPTA GUPTA Date: 2021.03.27 16:21:30 +0530 2 BRIEF STATEMENT OF FACTS FOR THE DECISION 1. Briefly, the case of complainant is that he is the proprietor of M/s Exotica Business Solution. The said company appointed accused as an Assistant Account Development Manager vide employment agreement dated 07.07.2016 for an annual salary of Rs. 7.5 lakh and additional performance acknowledgement incentive of Rs. 1.3 lakh. As a surety for employment agreement, the accused issued a cheque bearing no. 949955, dated 07.02.2017 for an amount of Rs. 2.4 lakh drawn on Punjab National Bank, Shivaji Park, Alwar, Rajasthan. The legal team of complainant sent an e-mail dated 01.02.2017 for recovery of amount of Rs.2.4 lakh to the accused but no response was received from her. Complainant presented the cheque for encashment twice and the same got dishonoured with remarks "FUNDS INSUFFICIENT" vide return memos dated 09.02.2017 and 18.03.2017. Complainant then sent legal demand notice dated 25.03.2017 to the accused, demanding payment of the amount of cheque in question within 15 days. The accused replied to the legal demand notice vide reply dated 07.04.2017 denying any liability towards the complainant. Since the amount of cheque in question was not paid by the accused within 15 days of receipt of the legal demand notice, the complainant has instituted the present proceedings under section 138 of the Negotiable Instruments Act, 1881 (hereinafter referred to as "the NI Act ").

case_name: Saurabh Kumar Banerjee vs . Pooja Agarwal
text: 1. Briefly, the case of complainant is that he is the proprietor of M/s Exotica Business Solution. The said company appointed accused as an Assistant Account Development Manager vide employment agreement dated 07.07.2016 for an annual salary of Rs. 7.5 lakh and additional performance acknowledgement incentive of Rs. 1.3 lakh. As a surety for employment agreement, the accused issued a cheque bearing no. 949955, dated 07.02.2017 for an amount of Rs. 2.4 lakh drawn on Punjab National Bank, Shivaji Park, Alwar, Rajasthan. The legal team of complainant sent an e-mail dated 01.02.2017 for recovery of amount of Rs.2.4 lakh to the accused but no response was received from her. Complainant presented the cheque for encashment twice and the same got dishonoured with remarks "FUNDS INSUFFICIENT" vide return memos dated 09.02.2017 and 18.03.2017. Complainant then sent legal demand notice dated 25.03.2017 to the accused, demanding payment of the amount of cheque in question within 15 days. The accused replied to the legal demand notice vide reply dated 07.04.2017 denying any liability towards the complainant. Since the amount of cheque in question was not paid by the accused within 15 days of receipt of the legal demand notice, the complainant has instituted the present proceedings under section 138 of the Negotiable Instruments Act, 1881 (hereinafter referred to as "the NI Act "). 2. The accused was summoned and after her appearance, she was served with notice under section 251 Criminal Procedure Code (hereinafter referred to as " Cr.P.C .") wherein the accused admitted the employment agreement, the signatures on the cheque in question, the receipt of legal demand notice but denied any liability towards the complainant and claimed that the cheque in question has been misused by the complainant. 3. The complainant examined himself as CW1. After her application under section 145(2) of the NI Act was allowed, the accused cross-examined the 3 complainant, wherein he reiterated his case. After complainant evidence was closed, statement of accused was recorded under section 313 Cr.P.C. Thereafter, the accused examined herself as defence witness under section 315 Cr.P.C. No other evidence was led on behalf of the accused. 4. Arguments were heard for both the parties and record of the case perused. Upon consideration of the same, this court observes as follows:

case_name: Saurabh Kumar Banerjee vs . Pooja Agarwal
text: complainant, wherein he reiterated his case. After complainant evidence was closed, statement of accused was recorded under section 313 Cr.P.C. Thereafter, the accused examined herself as defence witness under section 315 Cr.P.C. No other evidence was led on behalf of the accused. 4. Arguments were heard for both the parties and record of the case perused. Upon consideration of the same, this court observes as follows: a) Admittedly, the cheque in question was issued as a security for the employment agreement between the parties. This means that on the date of issuance of the cheque in question there was no existing legally enforceable liability. b) It is peculiar that nowhere in the complaint or his evidence on affidavit has the complainant disclosed as to how and when the amount mentioned in the cheque in question became due or payable. A suggestion is discernible from a perusal of Ex. CW1/C i.e. the email for recovery of Rs. 2.4 lakh sent by the legal team of the complainant to the accused. The said email mentions that the accused was on leave from 06.01.2017 to 09.01.2017 but she proceeded on No Call No Show leaves from 10.01.2017 to 24.01.2017. Thus, the complainant is claiming the amount of Rs. 2.4 lakh to have become due or payable as the accused was "absconding from work for more than 3 days without information", which was one of the conditions in the employment agreement for encashment of the cheque in question. c) It emerges that the liability alleged in the present case is in the nature of claim for damages for breach of employment agreement allegedly committed by the accused. The damages have been quantified in the agreement itself at Rs. 2.4 lakhs. The provision regarding recovery of damages for breach of contract, where a sum is named in the contract itself, is covered under section 74 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872. Such damages can be either in nature of liquidated sum of money, which is the genuine pre-estimate of loss anticipated to be suffered in case 4 of breach of contract or it can be stipulated by way of penalty, which is essentially in terrorem to deter the parties from committing breach of the contract.

case_name: Saurabh Kumar Banerjee vs . Pooja Agarwal
text: 4 of breach of contract or it can be stipulated by way of penalty, which is essentially in terrorem to deter the parties from committing breach of the contract. d) It is not the case of the complainant that the amount of Rs. 2.4 lakh has been mentioned in the employment agreement as liquidated damages or as a genuine pre-estimate of the loss anticipated to be suffered by employer due to the absence of the employee. Even if the same was so claimed, it is rather unjustifiable from a reasonable person's standard, that an employer would suffer loss of Rs. 2.4 lakh due to 3 days' absence or even 14 days' absence (from 10.01.2017 to 24.01.2017, as claimed in the email for recovery, Ex CW1/C) of an employee whose annual salary is about Rs. 7.5 lakhs. Thus, the amount of Rs. 2.4 lakhs in the employment agreement, which is quite unconscionable, is essentially in terrorem and the same is stipulated by way of 'penalty'. e) The crucial question that arises here is whether such a claim for the entire amount of sum stipulated by way of penalty can be said to be a legally enforceable liability so as to sustain the proceedings under section 138 NI Act? f) In the landmark decision cited as Fateh Chand vs. Balkishan Das ; 1964 SCR (1) 515, the constitution bench of the Hon'ble Supreme Court of India held that duty not to enforce penalty clause but only to award reasonable compensation is statutorily imposed upon the court by section 74 (referring to the Indian Contract Act, 1872 ). Thus, in case of breach of an agreement, a party is not entitled to recover the entire amount mentioned in the agreement as penalty but only to a reasonable compensation. When an agreement stipulates a penalty clause for breach and a cheque is issued at the time of execution of such agreement for securing the penalty clause, such that the amount of cheque equals the entire amount of penalty stipulated, the cheque is essentially for an amount exceeding a reasonable compensation. As such, in the event of breach of such an agreement, the entire amount of cheque does not become due or payable. Accordingly, such a 5 cheque cannot be said to have been issued in discharge of a legally enforceable liability.

case_name: Saurabh Kumar Banerjee vs . Pooja Agarwal
text: 5 cheque cannot be said to have been issued in discharge of a legally enforceable liability. g) Further, it has been recently laid down by a three judge bench of the Hon'ble Supreme Court of India in decision cited as P. Mohanraj & Ors. Vs. M/s Shah Brothers Ispat Pvt. Ltd. in Civil Appeal 10355 of 2018 , date of decision 01.03.2021, that section 138 NI Act is really a hybrid provision to enforce payment under a bounced cheque if it is otherwise enforceable in civil law. Since penalty clauses, in entirety, are not enforceable even under civil law, the necessary conclusion is that any cheque issued at the time of execution of an agreement for securing the penalty clause cannot be used to enforce the payment of the entire amount of penalty. Therefore, if such a cheque is dishonoured then provision under section 138 NI Act cannot be used to enforce payment thereunder which is not legally due or enforceable. h) In view of the above analysis and also the fact that the complainant has not disclosed anywhere that on what basis he is claiming the entire amount of penalty to have become due or payable, this court holds that the cheque in question being issued for securing the penalty clause of the employment agreement, the same cannot be said to have been issued in discharge of a legally enforceable liability and therefore, proceedings under section 138 NI Act cannot be used to enforce payment thereunder. i) Next, in order to establish existence of a legally enforceable liability, the complainant has placed reliance on the decision of the Hon'ble High Court of Delhi cited as Capt. Deepak Verma Vs. Pawan Hans Helicopters Ltd. ; 2018(3) AD(Delhi) 690. The reliance is misplaced as the said decision dealt with recovery of liquidated damages upon an indemnity bond for service contract in a civil suit and has no application to the present proceedings under section 138 of NI Act. j) Lastly, the complainant has contended that since the accused has admitted her signatures on the cheque in question, the complainant is not required to prove 6 the existence of a legally enforceable liability. It is contended that legally enforceable liability shall be presumed under section 139 of the NI Act and it is incumbent upon the accused to rebut the same.

case_name: Saurabh Kumar Banerjee vs . Pooja Agarwal
text: 6 the existence of a legally enforceable liability. It is contended that legally enforceable liability shall be presumed under section 139 of the NI Act and it is incumbent upon the accused to rebut the same. k) In the decision cited as Krishna Janardhan Bhat v. Dattatraya G. Hegde ; AIR 2008 SC 1325, the Hon'ble Supreme Court of India held as follows: "....20. Section 138 of the Act has three ingredients, viz.: (i) that there is a legally enforceable debt; (ii)that the cheque was drawn from the account of bank for discharge in whole or in part of any debt or other liability which pre- supposes a legally enforceable debt; and (iii)that the cheque so issued had been returned due to insufficiency of funds. 21. The proviso appended to the said section provides for compliance of legal requirements before a complaint petition can be acted upon by a court of law. Section 139 of the Act merely raises a presumption in regard to the second aspect of the matter. Existence of legally recoverable debt is not a matter of presumption under Section 139 of the Act. It merely raises a presumption in favour of a holder of the cheque that the same has been issued for discharge of any debt or other liability......" Thus, the settled position of law is that before a court sets about raising presumption under section 139 of the NI Act and delves into its rebuttal, first and foremost it is imperative to examine whether the case of the complainant can even stand on its own legs by establishing the legal requirements of section 138 NI Act. The existence of a legally enforceable liability is one of the three legal requirements to be fulfilled by the complainant. Until this is proved by the complainant, a complaint under section 138 NI Act cannot be sustained. Thus, if from the pleadings and the evidence of complainant, no legally enforceable liability is made out, the presumption under section 139 NI Act will not come to the rescue of the complainant and facilitate him to enforce the cheque for an amount which is not legally due or enforceable. Therefore, the last contention of the accused is also not tenable, hence rejected. 7

case_name: Saurabh Kumar Banerjee vs . Pooja Agarwal
text: Thus, the settled position of law is that before a court sets about raising presumption under section 139 of the NI Act and delves into its rebuttal, first and foremost it is imperative to examine whether the case of the complainant can even stand on its own legs by establishing the legal requirements of section 138 NI Act. The existence of a legally enforceable liability is one of the three legal requirements to be fulfilled by the complainant. Until this is proved by the complainant, a complaint under section 138 NI Act cannot be sustained. Thus, if from the pleadings and the evidence of complainant, no legally enforceable liability is made out, the presumption under section 139 NI Act will not come to the rescue of the complainant and facilitate him to enforce the cheque for an amount which is not legally due or enforceable. Therefore, the last contention of the accused is also not tenable, hence rejected. 7 5. In view of the foregoing findings, this court holds that the case of the complainant collapses for want of a legally enforceable liability. The complainant has failed to prove the offence under section 138 NI Act against the accused. Consequently, the accused Ms. Pooja Aggarwal is acquitted of the offence under section 138 of the Negotiable Instruments Act, 1881. Pronounced in the (SWATI GUPTA) open court on 27.03.2021 MM-04/NIAct/South/Saket New Delhi

case_name: M/S. Sirivanthae Chits Pvt vs Sri. J.P. Santhosh Kumar
text: Bangalore District Court M/S. Sirivanthae Chits Pvt vs Sri. J.P. Santhosh Kumar on 21 October, 2021 IN THE COURT OF THE XXVII ADDL., CHIEF METROPOLITAN MAGISTRATE, BANGALORE. Present: Sri. T. GOVINDAIAH. B.Com., LLB., Dated: This the 21st Day of October 2021 C.C.No.24855/2018 Complainant M/S. SIRIVANTHAE CHITS PVT, LTD., No.2, 14th Main, West of Chord Road, Mahalakshmipuram, Bangalore-560 086. Represented by its Recovery Officer, Sri. M.N. Arunkumar, S/o. Sri. M. Late B. Narayan, Aged about 45 Years. (Rep by Sri. H.V. Shiva Kumar., Adv.) Accused SRI. J.P. SANTHOSH KUMAR, Aged about 39 Years, S/o. Sri. Puttaraju, R/a. No. 53/20, K.N. Bhaskar Reddy Building, Govindashetty Palya, Electronic City, Bengaluru 560100. (Rep by Sri. R.K, Adv) Offence U/s.138 of Negotiable Instruments Act . Plea of the accused Pleaded not guilty Final Order Convicted Judgment Date 21/10/2021 2 C.C. No. 24855/2018 JUDGMENT The complainant Chits company filed this complaint against the accused for the offence punishable U/s.138 of the Negotiable Instruments Act . 2. Brief facts of the complainant case are as follows: The complainant company is a registered Chit company. The accused subscribed to Chit bearing No.SCPLLT-01, Ticket No.38 for chit value of Rs.5,00,000/- conducted by the complainant company and the said Chit Group launched on 07/11/2014 and closed on 08/02/2018. The accused bid the said chit and has received the Chit amount of Rs.3,00,000/- from the complainant by executing the Chit documents. After obtaining prize money the accused failed to pay the monthly Chit installments and he has became over due. The complainant demanded the accused to pay 4 monthly Chit installments amounting to Rs.50,000/- and penalty of Rs.01,972.60, total a sum of Rs.51,973/- and the accused 3 C.C.No.24855/2018 issued cheque No. 364779 dated: 07/03/2018 for Rs.51,973/- drawn on State Bank of Mysore, Hirisave Branch, towards repayment of the chit installments. The complainant presented the said cheque on 03/04/2018 for realization through PMC Bank Ltd., Mahalakshmi Layout Branch, Bengaluru, but the said cheque dishonoured as per the bankers memo dated: 04/04/2018 for the reason "Funds Insufficient". Thereafter, the complainant company issued legal notice dated: 28/04//2018 through RPAD and the said notice returned on 05/05/2018 with an endorsement "LEFT, RETURNED TO SENDER". The accused has failed to pay the cheque amount. Hence, this complaint. 3. Sworn statement of Recovery officer and Authorized representative of the complainant company recorded as PW1 and got marked Ex.P1 to 13 documents

case_name: M/S. Sirivanthae Chits Pvt vs Sri. J.P. Santhosh Kumar
text: Authorized representative of the complainant company recorded as PW1 and got marked Ex.P1 to 13 documents and taking cognizance for the offence U/sec. 138 of the Negotiable Instruments Act ordered summons to the 4 C.C.No.24855/2018 accused. The accused appeared through his advocate and he enlarged on bail. Substance of accusation readover to the accused. To which accused pleads not guilty and claims to be tried. In view of the decision of the Hon'ble Supreme Court of India reported in (2014) 5 SCC 590 in the case of Indian Bank Association & Others V/s Union of India & Others, sworn statement of the complainant is treated as chief examination of complainant. PW1 has led further evidence and got marked 1 document as per Ex.P14 and one witness namely C.R. Venkatesh (Postman) S/o. Late K. Ramalingareddy has been examined as PW2 and got marked Ex.P15 document and closed their side. The accused has filed an application U/s.145(2) of the NI Act seeking permission for cross- examination of the complainant and the said application was allowed. 4. On closure of the complainant side evidence statement of the accused recorded U/sec. 313 of Cr.P.C ,. 5 C.C.No.24855/2018 The accused has denied all the incriminating materials appeared in the evidence of PW1 as false. The accused has claimed for defense evidence. The accused himself examined as DW1 and got marked Ex.D1 document and closed his side. 5. Heard the arguments on both the sides and perused the materials produced by both the parties. 6. The points that would arise for my consideration are as follows: (i) Whether the complainant proves that the accused issued Ex.P.10 cheque towards discharge of legally enforcible debt or liability and the said cheque dishonoured.? (ii) Whether the complainant proves that after dishonour of cheque, issued notice to the accused in compliance of Sec.138(b) of N.I Act? (iii) What order? 6 C.C.No.24855/2018 7. My answer to the above points are as follows:- Point No.1 : In the Affirmative Point No.2 : In the Affirmative Point No.3: As per the final order, for the following. REASONS 8. Point No.1 : It is the case of the complainant that, the complainant company is a registered Chit company. The accused subscribed to Chit bearing No.SCPLLT-01, Ticket No.38 for chit value of Rs.5,00,000/- conducted by the complainant company and the said Chit Group launched on 07/11/2014 and closed on

case_name: M/S. Sirivanthae Chits Pvt vs Sri. J.P. Santhosh Kumar
text: conducted by the complainant company and the said Chit Group launched on 07/11/2014 and closed on 08/02/2018. The accused bid the said chit and has received the Chit amount of Rs.3,00,000/- from the complainant by executing the Chit documents. After obtaining prize money the accused failed to pay the monthly Chit installments and he has became over due. The complainant demanded the accused to pay 4 monthly Chit installments amounting to Rs.50,000/- and penalty of 7 C.C.No.24855/2018 Rs.01,972.60, total a sum of Rs.51,973/- and the accused issued cheque No. 364779 dated: 07/03/2018 for Rs.51,973/- drawn on State Bank of Mysore, Hirisave Branch, towards repayment of the chit installments. The complainant presented the said cheque on 03/04/2018 for realization through PMC Bank Ltd., Mahalakshmi Layout Branch, Bengaluru, but the said cheque dishonoured as per the bankers memo dated: 04/04/2018 for the reason "Funds Insufficient". 9. The accused being the subscriber of Chit conducted by the complainant for Rs.5,00,000/-. The accused being the bidder of Chit, he received Rs.3,00,000/- from the complainant and executed documents infavour of the complainant. But, he fails to pay the installments of Chit amount. Thereafter, the complainant has demanded to pay the 4 monthly Chit installments amounting to Rs.50,000/- along with penalty of Rs.01,972.60. To which the accused issued cheque bearing No. 364779 for 8 C.C.No.24855/2018 Rs.51,973/- on 07/03/2018. On the assurance of the accused the complainant presented the said cheque for encashment on 03/04/2018. But the said cheque was dishonored for the reason "Funds Insufficient" on 04/04/2018. Accordingly the accused committed the offence U/sec. 138 of the Negotiable Instruments Act . The accused also not denied the subscriber of Chit and he also not denied the issuance of Rs.51,973/-. 10. It is important to note that, the accused also not disputed issuance of cheque infavour of the complainant and he also not disputed his signature on Ex.P10 Cheque. Here in this case, it is an admitted fact that, the complainant company is running Chit Fund business and it is also an admitted fact that the accused is subscriber of Chit. Accordingly, the accused being the subscriber of Chit issued Ex.P10 infavour the complainant for discharge of legally enforcible debts. 9 C.C.No.24855/2018 11. It is important to note that, in the cross- examination of DW1 is clearly admits that he issued Ex.P10 infavour of complainant for discharge of debts.

case_name: M/S. Sirivanthae Chits Pvt vs Sri. J.P. Santhosh Kumar
text: examination of DW1 is clearly admits that he issued Ex.P10 infavour of complainant for discharge of debts. Therefore, the evidence of PW1 is clearly corroborated with Ex.P1 to 11. Therefore, presumption is U/sec. 139 of the N.I. Act would raises infavour of the complainant. In view of the above discussion, this court answer Point No.1 in the Affirmative. 12. Point No.2:- It is the case of the complainant that, after dishonor of the Ex.P10 Cheque, issued notice to the accused on 28/04/2018 through RPAD and same has been returned as the accused "LEFT, RETURNED TO SENDER". The accused contended that till today he residing in the same address and he continued his business by residing with family members. 10 C.C.No.24855/2018 13. Further, the complainant has been examined one witness namely C.R. Venkatesh, he working as Postman as PW2. In the evidence of PW2, he clearly states he visited the address mentioned in the postal cover. But, the address of the accused not traced. Upon enquiry he confirmed that the accused "LEFT, RETURNED TO SENDER". and he mentioned the same on postal cover. The accused took various defense by denying the issuance of notice. But, the complainant produced the Ex.P13 to 15, wherein the postal authority is clearly mentioned as "LEFT, RETURNED TO SENDER". Under such circumstances there is no more scope in the defense of the accused. Because, there may be chance to the accused intentionally avoiding to take notice. Therefore, the complainant has produced the sufficient materials to show that he issued notice dated: 28/04/2018 after dishonour of the cheque. 14. It is important to note that, in the evidence of DW1, he also states that he residing in the same place. But 11 C.C.No.24855/2018 the accused opened his Bank account at Hirisave Branch, Hassan District. It go to show that, accused also having its residential address in Hassan District, but not mentioned his address in any document. Therefore, the contention of the accused not holding any water. When the accused himself admits about issuance of Ex.P10 and the said cheque was dishonoured as per Bank endorsement i.e., Ex.P11. Therefore, materials produced by the complainant clearly go to show that after dishonour of cheque the complainant send a notice to the accused and the said notice unserved to the accused. However, the accused appeared in this case. Therefore, the complainant has complied the mandatory requirement U/sec. 138(b) of the

case_name: M/S. Sirivanthae Chits Pvt vs Sri. J.P. Santhosh Kumar
text: appeared in this case. Therefore, the complainant has complied the mandatory requirement U/sec. 138(b) of the Negotiable Instruments Act . 15. It is relevant here to refer the decisions reported in 2001 AIR Karnataka HCR 2154 between 'M/s.Devi Tyres V/s.Navab Jan' and in 2011 ACD 1521 (KAR) between 'Smt. Usha Suresh V/s. Shashidharn', in 2010 12 C.C.No.24855/2018 SC 1898 between 'Rangappa Vs. Mohan ' and 2011 ACD 1412 (KAR) between 'N.Hasainar Vs. M.Hasainar , S/o. Ibrahim'. The Hon'ble High Court of Karnataka in the above decision i.e., 2001 AIR Karnataka HCR 2154 at para No.6 was pleased to hold that issuance of cheque itself was adequate proof of existence of debt or liability. The Hon'ble Apex Court has also held in the decision referred above , the Hon'ble Apex Court in 2010 SC 1898 in case of 'Rangappa Vs. Mohan ' that, presumptions U/ sec.118(a) and 139 of N.I. Act indeed does extend to the existence of legally recoverable debt, of course the said presumption is rebuttable one, the accused has to rebut the presumption by taking probable defence. In another decision of Hon'ble Apex court of India reported in CRIMINAL APPEAL NO. 508 OF 2018 DT 15-03-2018 between ROHITBHAI JIVANLAL PATEL Vs STATE OF GUJARAT AND ANR held that " Negotiable Instruments Act facts like source of funds are not relevant if the Accused has not been able to 13 C.C.No.24855/2018 rebut the presumption. It is further held that "When such a presumption is drawn, the facts relating to the want of documentary evidence in the form of receipts or accounts or want of evidence as regards source of funds were not of relevant consideration while examining if the Accused has been able to rebut the presumption or not". In another decision of Hon'ble High Court of Karnataka reported in ILR 2019 KAR 493 in the case of Sri.Yogesh Poojary Vs. Sri.K.Shankara Bhat , wherein the Hon'ble High Court held that, the presumption mandated by Sec.139 of N.I Act includes the presumption that, there existed a legally enforceable debt or liability, however such presumption is rebuttable in nature". In another decision of Hon'ble High Court of Karnataka in the case of Shri.V.R.Shresti Vs. Shri. Bhaskara.P. in Crl. Appeal No. 2109/2017 dated: 15.10.2019 wherein the Hon'ble High Court of Karnataka held that "the Accused has not 14 C.C.No.24855/2018 given any reply to the notice and also in the cross-

case_name: M/S. Sirivanthae Chits Pvt vs Sri. J.P. Santhosh Kumar
text: 14 C.C.No.24855/2018 given any reply to the notice and also in the cross- examination, he categorically admits that, the cheque has bounced on account of no sufficient fund in the bank account of the Accused. Mere non producing of the document before the court with regard to the source of income to advance a loan is not a ground to dismiss the complaint. The Accused ought to have rebutted the contention of the complainant by producing cogent evidence before the court and mere denial is not enough". In another decision of Hon'ble Apex Court of India reported in ICL 2021(2) SC 529 in the case of M/s. Kalamani Tex Vs. P.Balasubramanian , dt: 10.02.2021 wherein the Hon'ble Apex court held that " once the accused had admitted his signatures on the cheque and deed, the trial court ought to have presumed that, the cheque was issued as consideration for legally enforceable debt". Hence the principles of law laid down by the Hon'ble 15 C.C.No.24855/2018 Apex Court of India and Hon'ble High Court of Karnataka in the above referred decisions are aptly applicable to the case on hand, since in the present case also the complainant has proved that, the cheque in question belonging to the account of the Accused and signature appearing on the cheque is that of the Accused and it was issued to the complainant, in such circumstances, this court has drawn presumption of existence of legally enforceable debt as per Sec.139 of the Negotiable Instruments Act. 16. Therefore, considering all these aspects of the case and totality of the circumstances and careful perusal of the evidence adduced by both the parties, the court is of the opinion that the accused being the subscriber of the Chit and issued Ex.P10 Cheque for discharge of legal debt of Rs.51,973/-. Therefore complainant has successfully established beyond all reasonable doubts that the accused being the subscriber of Chit and issued Ex.P10 to discharge legal debt of Rs.51,973/-. The complainant issued legal 16 C.C.No.24855/2018 notice dated: 28/04/2018 and the same was returned as accused "LEFT, RETURNED TO SENDER". It go to show that the accused avoided to take the notice. Therefore, the accused has committed an offence punishable U/sec. 138 of the N.I. Act . Accordingly, this court answered Point No. 2 in the Affirmative. 17. Point No.3 :- In view of the above findings on the

case_name: M/S. Sirivanthae Chits Pvt vs Sri. J.P. Santhosh Kumar
text: in the Affirmative. 17. Point No.3 :- In view of the above findings on the Point Nos.1 and 2 the complainant proved that, the accused issued Ex.P10. Cheque towards discharge of legal debts and the said cheque was dishonoured and the complainant has complied the mandatory requirement of section 138(b) of the NI Act. Therefore, the accused is liable for punishment for the offence punishable U/s. 138 of the NI Act . In cheque bounce cases apart from punishment to the accused compensation can also be awarded to the complainant with respect to dishonoured cheque. Here is a case, Ex.P.10 cheque is dated 07/03/2018 and the amount 17 C.C.No.24855/2018 covered there under is of Rs.51,973/-. The act of the accused dragged the complainant to the court and the complainant spent more than 3 years for redressal and the accused made the complainant to incur expenses. Under these circumstances, the complainant has to be suitably compensated in the matter and in the result this court proceed pass the following:- ORDER The complaint filed by the complainant Chit company against the accused is allowed. Acting U/s.255(2) of Cr.P.C ., the accused convicted for the offence punishable U/s.138 of N.I. Act and sentenced to pay fine of Rs.60,000/-. In default of payment of said fine amount, the accused shall undergo simple imprisonment for a period of Three months. Out of the said fine amount ordered to pay Rs.55,000/- to the complainant as compensation and the remaining amount of Rs.5,000/- shall go to the state. 18 C.C.No.24855/2018 (Dictated to the stenographer, transcribed by her, corrected and then pronounced in open court by me on this the 21st day of October, 2021) (T. case_name: M/S. Sirivanthae Chits Pvt vs Sri. J.P. Santhosh Kumar
text: Bangalore District Court M/S. Sirivanthae Chits Pvt vs Sri. J.P. Santhosh Kumar on 21 October, 2021 IN THE COURT OF THE XXVII ADDL., CHIEF METROPOLITAN MAGISTRATE, BANGALORE. Present: Sri. T. GOVINDAIAH. B.Com., LLB., Dated: This the 21st Day of October 2021 C.C.No.24854/2018 Complainant M/S. SIRIVANTHAE CHITS PVT, LTD., No.2, 14th Main, West of Chord Road, Mahalakshmipuram, Bangalore-560 086. Represented by its Recovery Officer, Sri. M.N. Arunkumar, S/o. Sri. M. Late B. Narayan, Aged about 45 Years. (Rep by Sri. H.V. Shiva Kumar., Adv.) V/s. Accused SRI. J.P. SANTHOSH KUMAR, Aged about 39 Years, S/o. Sri. Puttaraju, R/a. No. 53/20, K.N. Bhaskar Reddy Building, Govindashetty Palya, Electronic City, Bengaluru 560100. (Rep by Sri. R.K, Adv) Offence U/s.138 of Negotiable Instruments Act . Plea of the accused Pleaded not guilty Final Order Convicted Judgment Date 21/10/2021 2 C.C. No. 24854/2018 JUDGMENT The complainant Chits company filed this complaint against the accused for the offence punishable U/s.138 of the Negotiable Instruments Act . 2. Brief facts of the complainant case are as follows: The complainant company is a registered Chit company. The accused subscribed to Chit bearing No.SCPLLT-01, Ticket No.39 for chit value of Rs.5,00,000/- conducted by the complainant company and the said Chit Group launched on 07/11/2014 and closed on 08/02/2018. The accused bid the said chit and has received the Chit amount of Rs.3,00,000/- from the complainant by executing the Chit documents. After obtaining prize money the accused failed to pay the monthly Chit installments and he has became over due. The complainant demanded the accused to pay 5 monthly Chit installments amounting to Rs.62,500/- and penalty of 3 C.C.No.24854/2018 Rs.03,102.74, total a sum of Rs.65,102.74 and the accused issued cheque No. 364769 dated: 07/03/2018 for Rs.65,603/- drawn on State Bank of Mysore, Hirisave Branch, towards repayment of the chit installments. The complainant presented the said cheque on 03/04/2018 for realization through PMC Bank Ltd., Mahalakshmi Layout Branch, Bengaluru, but the said cheque dishonoured as per the bankers memo dated: 04/04/2018 for the reason "Funds Insufficient". Thereafter, the complainant company issued legal notice dated: 03/05//2018 through RPAD and the said notice returned on 05/05/2018 with an endorsement "LEFT". The accused has failed to pay the cheque amount. Hence, this complaint. 3. Sworn statement of Recovery officer and Authorized representative of the complainant company recorded as PW1 and got marked Ex.P1 to 13 documents

case_name: M/S. Sirivanthae Chits Pvt vs Sri. J.P. Santhosh Kumar
text: Authorized representative of the complainant company recorded as PW1 and got marked Ex.P1 to 13 documents and taking cognizance for the offence U/sec. 138 of the 4 C.C.No.24854/2018 Negotiable Instruments Act ordered summons to the accused. The accused appeared through his advocate and he enlarged on bail. Substance of accusation readover to the accused. To which accused pleads not guilty and claims to be tried. In view of the decision of the Hon'ble Supreme Court of India reported in (2014) 5 SCC 590 in the case of Indian Bank Association & Others V/s Union of India & Others, sworn statement of the complainant is treated as chief examination of complainant. PW1 has led further evidence and got marked 1 document as per Ex.P14 and one witness namely C.R. Venkatesh (Postman) S/o. Late K. Ramalingareddy has been examined as PW2 and got marked Ex.P15 document and closed their side. The accused has filed an application U/s.145(2) of the NI Act seeking permission for cross- examination of the complainant and the said application was allowed. 5 C.C.No.24854/2018 4. On closure of the complainant side evidence statement of the accused recorded U/sec. 313 of Cr.P.C ,. The accused has denied all the incriminating materials appeared in the evidence of PW1 as false. The accused has claimed for defense evidence. The accused himself examined as DW1 and got marked Ex.D1 document and closed his side. 5. Heard the arguments on both the sides and perused the materials produced by both the parties. 6. The points that would arise for my consideration are as follows: (i) Whether the complainant proves that the accused issued Ex.P.10 cheque towards discharge of legally enforcible debt or liability and the said cheque dishonoured.? (ii) Whether the complainant proves that after dishonour of 6 C.C.No.24854/2018 cheque, issued notice to the accused in compliance of Sec.138(b) of N.I Act? (iii) What order? 7. My answer to the above points are as follows:- Point No.1 : In the Affirmative Point No.2 : In the Affirmative Point No.3: As per the final order, for the following. REASONS 8. Point No.1 : It is the case of the complainant that, the complainant company is a registered Chit company. The accused subscribed to Chit bearing No.SCPLLT-01, Ticket No.39 for chit value of Rs.5,00,000/- conducted by the complainant company and the said Chit Group launched on 07/11/2014 and closed on

case_name: M/S. Sirivanthae Chits Pvt vs Sri. J.P. Santhosh Kumar
text: conducted by the complainant company and the said Chit Group launched on 07/11/2014 and closed on 08/02/2018. The accused bid the said chit and has received the Chit amount of Rs.3,00,000/- from the complainant by executing the Chit documents. After 7 C.C.No.24854/2018 obtaining prize money the accused failed to pay the monthly Chit installments and he has became over due. The complainant demanded the accused to pay 5 monthly Chit installments amounting to Rs.62,500/- and penalty of Rs.03,102.74, total a sum of Rs.65,102.74 and the accused issued cheque No. 364769 dated: 07/03/2018 for Rs.65,603/- drawn on State Bank of Mysore, Hirisave Branch, towards repayment of the chit installments. The complainant presented the said cheque on 03/04/2018 for realization through PMC Bank Ltd., Mahalakshmi Layout Branch, Bengaluru, but the said cheque dishonoured as per the bankers memo dated: 04/04/2018 for the reason "Funds Insufficient". 9. The accused being the subscriber of Chit conducted by the complainant for Rs.5,00,000/-. The accused being the bidder of Chit, he received Rs.3,00,000/- from the complainant and executed documents infavour of 8 C.C.No.24854/2018 the complainant. But, he fails to pay the installments of Chit amount. Thereafter, the complainant has demanded to pay the 5 monthly Chit installments amounting to Rs.62,500/- along with penalty of Rs.3,102.74. To which the accused issued cheque bearing No. 364769 for Rs.65,603/- on 07/03/2018. On the assurance of the accused the complainant presented the said cheque for encashment on 03/04/2018. But the said cheque was dishonored for the reason "Funds Insufficient" on 04/04/2018. Accordingly the accused committed the offence U/sec. 138 of the Negotiable Instruments Act . The accused also not denied the subscriber of Chit and he also not denied the issuance of Rs.65,603/-. 10. It is important to note that, the accused also not disputed issuance of cheque infavour of the complainant and he also not disputed his signature on Ex.P10 Cheque. 9 C.C.No.24854/2018 Here in this case, it is an admitted fact that, the complainant company is running Chit Fund business and it is also an admitted fact that the accused is subscriber of Chit. Accordingly, the accused being the subscriber of Chit issued Ex.P10 infavour the complainant for discharge of legally enforcible debts. 11. It is important to note that, in the cross- examination of DW1 is clearly admits that he issued Ex.P10 infavour of complainant for discharge of debts.

case_name: M/S. Sirivanthae Chits Pvt vs Sri. J.P. Santhosh Kumar
text: examination of DW1 is clearly admits that he issued Ex.P10 infavour of complainant for discharge of debts. Therefore, the evidence of PW1 is clearly corroborated with Ex.P1 to 11. Therefore, presumption is U/sec. 139 of the N.I. Act would raises infavour of the complainant. In view of the above discussion, I answer Point No.1 in the Affirmative. 12. Point No.2:- It is the case of the complainant that, after dishonor of the Ex.P10 Cheque, issued notice to 10 C.C.No.24854/2018 the accused on 03/05/2018 through RPAD and same has been returned as the accused left the address. The accused contended that till today he residing in the same address and he continued his business by residing with family members. 13. Further, the complainant has been examined one witness namely C.R. Venkatesh, he working as Postman as PW2. In the evidence of PW2, he clearly states he visited the address mentioned in the postal cover. But, the address of the accused not traced. Upon enquiry he confirmed that the accused left the address and he mentioned the same on postal cover. The accused took various defense by denying the issuance of notice. But, the complainant produced the Ex.P13 & 14, wherein the postal authority is clearly mentioned as Left the Address. Under such circumstances there is no more scope in the defense of the accused. Because, there may be chance to the accused 11 C.C.No.24854/2018 intentionally avoiding to take notice. Therefore, the complainant has produced the sufficient materials to show that he issued notice dated: 03/05/2018 after dishonour of the cheque. 14. It is important to note that, in the evidence of DW1, he also states that he residing in the same place. But the accused opened his Bank account at Hirisave Branch, Hassan District. It go to show that, accused also having its residential address in Hassan District, but not mentioned his address in any document. Therefore, the contention of the accused not holding any water. When the accused himself admits about issuance of Ex.P10 and the said cheque was dishonoured as per Bank endorsement i.e., Ex.P11. Therefore, materials produced by the complainant clearly go to show that after dishonour of cheque the complainant send a notice to the accused and the said notice unserved to the accused. However, the accused 12 C.C.No.24854/2018 appeared in this case. Therefore, the complainant has complied the mandatory requirement U/sec. 138(b) of the

case_name: M/S. Sirivanthae Chits Pvt vs Sri. J.P. Santhosh Kumar
text: appeared in this case. Therefore, the complainant has complied the mandatory requirement U/sec. 138(b) of the Negotiable Instruments Act . 15. Shashidharn', in 2010 SC 1898 between 'Rangappa Vs. Mohan ' and 2011 ACD 1412 (KAR) between 'N.Hasainar Vs. M.Hasainar , S/o. Ibrahim'. The Hon'ble High Court of Karnataka in the above decision i.e., 2001 AIR Karnataka HCR 2154 at para No.6 was pleased to hold that issuance of cheque itself was adequate proof of existence of debt or liability. The Hon'ble Apex Court has also held in the decision referred above , the Hon'ble Apex Court in 2010 SC 1898 in case of 'Rangappa Vs. Mohan ' that, presumptions U/ sec.118(a) and 139 of N.I. Act indeed does extend to the existence of 13 C.C.No.24854/2018 legally recoverable debt, of course the said presumption is rebuttable one, the accused has to rebut the presumption by taking probable defence. In another decision of Hon'ble Apex court of India reported in CRIMINAL APPEAL NO. 508 OF 2018 DT 15-03-2018 between ROHITBHAI JIVANLAL PATEL Vs STATE OF GUJARAT AND ANR held that " Negotiable Instruments Act facts like source of funds are not relevant if the Accused has not been able to rebut the presumption. It is further held that "When such a presumption is drawn, the facts relating to the want of documentary evidence in the form of receipts or accounts or want of evidence as regards source of funds were not of relevant consideration while examining if the Accused has been able to rebut the presumption or not". In another decision of Hon'ble High Court of Karnataka reported in ILR 2019 KAR 493 in the case of Sri.Yogesh Poojary Vs. Sri.K.Shankara Bhat , 14 C.C.No.24854/2018 wherein the Hon'ble High Court held that, the presumption mandated by Sec.139 of N.I Act includes the presumption that, there existed a legally enforceable debt or liability, however such presumption is rebuttable in nature". In another decision of Hon'ble High Court of Karnataka in the case of Shri.V.R.Shresti Vs. Shri. Bhaskara.P. in Crl. Appeal No. 2109/2017 dated: 15.10.2019 wherein the Hon'ble High Court of Karnataka held that "the Accused has not given any reply to the notice and also in the cross-

case_name: M/S. Sirivanthae Chits Pvt vs Sri. J.P. Santhosh Kumar
text: High Court of Karnataka held that "the Accused has not given any reply to the notice and also in the cross- examination, he categorically admits that, the cheque has bounced on account of no sufficient fund in the bank account of the Accused. Mere non producing of the document before the court with regard to the source of income to advance a loan is not a ground to dismiss the complaint. The Accused ought to have rebutted the contention of the complainant by 15 C.C.No.24854/2018 producing cogent evidence before the court and mere denial is not enough". In another decision of Hon'ble Apex Court of India reported in ICL 2021(2) SC 529 in the case of M/s. Kalamani Tex Vs. P.Balasubramanian , dt: 10.02.2021 wherein the Hon'ble Apex court held that " once the accused had admitted his signatures on the cheque and deed, the trial court ought to have presumed that, the cheque was issued as consideration for legally enforceable debt". Hence the principles of law laid down by the Hon'ble Apex Court of India and Hon'ble High Court of Karnataka in the above referred decisions are aptly applicable to the case on hand, since in the present case also the complainant has proved that, the cheque in question belonging to the account of the Accused and signature appearing on the cheque is that of the Accused and it was issued to the complainant, in such circumstances, this court has drawn 16 C.C.No.24854/2018 presumption of existence of legally enforceable debt as per Sec.139 of the Negotiable Instruments Act. 16. Therefore, considering all these aspects of the case and totality of the circumstances and careful perusal of the evidence adduced by both the parties, the court is of the opinion that the accused being the subscriber of the Chit and issued Ex.P10 Cheque for discharge of legal debt of Rs.65,603/-. Therefore complainant has successfully established beyond all reasonable doubts that the accused being the subscriber of Chit and issued Ex.P10 to discharge legal debt of Rs.65,603/-. The complainant issued legal notice dated: 03/05/2018 and the same was returned as accused Left the Address. It go to show that the accused avoided to take the notice. Therefore, the accused has committed an offence punishable U/sec. 138 of the N.I. Act . Accordingly, this court answered Point No. 2 in the Affirmative. 17 C.C.No.24854/2018

text: Affirmative. 17 C.C.No.24854/2018 17. Point No.3 :- In view of the above findings on the Point Nos.1 and 2 the complainant proved that, the accused issued Ex.P10. Cheque towards discharge of legal debts and the said cheque was dishonoured and the complainant has complied the mandatory requirement of section 138(b) of the NI Act. Therefore, the accused is liable for punishment for the offence punishable U/s. 138 of the NI Act . Here is a case, Ex.P.10 cheque is dated 07/03/2018 and the amount covered there under is of Rs.65,603/-. Under these circumstances, the complainant has to be suitably 18 C.C.No.24854/2018 compensated in the matter and in the result this court proceed pass the following:- ORDER The complaint filed by the complainant Chit company against the accused is allowed. case_name: Joginder Pal Salhotra vs Mangat Singh
text: Delhi District Court Joginder Pal Salhotra vs Mangat Singh on 7 August, 2024 THE COURT OF SHRI VAIBHAV PRATAP SINGH JFMC (NI ACT) DIGITAL COURT - 02, WEST DISTRICT TIS HAZARI COURTS, DELHI CC NI Act No. 3634/2021 Joginder Pal Salhotra v. Mangat Singh Unique QR Code Case Number CC NI Act No. 3634/2021 Details of Complainant Joginder Pal Salhotra S/o Late Sh. Kasturi Lal Salhotra Aged about 49 years R/o H. No. C-78, Vishnu Garden New Delhi - 110018 (M) 9718410410 Details of Accused Mangat Singh S/o Sh. Jagdish Ram Aged about 54 years R/o H. No. J-20, Upper Ground 3rd Floor, Vishnu Garden New Delhi-110018 Case Number Record (CNR) DLWT020208662021 Date of Filing 09.12.2021 Offence Complained of Section 138 NI Act, 1881 Police Station Tilak Nagar Plea of Accused Not Guilty Final Decision Acquittal Final Arguments Concluded on 24.07.2024 Date of Decision 07.08.2024 Digitally signed VAIBHAV by VAIBHAV PRATAP SINGH PRATAP Date: SINGH 2024.08.07 16:27:43 +0530 CC NI Act No.3634/2021 DLWT020208662021 JUDGMENT (BRIEF STATEMENT OF REASONS FOR DECISION) 1. This is a private complaint case for bouncing of cheque seeking to punish the offence under Section 138 of the Negotiable Instruments Act, 1881 (NI Act ). ALLEGATIONS IN THE COMPLAINT 2. Briefly put, the allegations in the Complaint are that Complainant and Accused Person were having good friendly relations from last many years. The Accused Person approached the Complainant for a friendly loan of INR 2,70,000/- for the medical treatment of his mother, which the Complainant gave to him on 20.09.2018. Accused Person in discharge of his liability issued a post dated cheque bearing No. 568640 dated 01.10.2021 for INR 2,70,000/- drawn on State Bank of India, Khyala, Delhi Branch which got returned dishonoured on 08.10.2021 due to reason "Kindly Contact Drawer/ Drawee Bank and please present again". Despite sending the statutory notice dated 21.10.2021, no payment was received and thus the present proceedings were initiated. SUMMONING OF ACCUSED AND FRAMING OF NOTICE U/S 251 CrPC 3. Accused Person admitted the signatures on the cheque but denied filling up the rest of its particulars. Receipt of the statutory notice of demand was admitted. 4. After receiving notice of accusation, the Accused Person disclosed his defence wherein he stated thus: "I do not personally know the complainant and we are definitely not friends. I have never even spoken to the complainant on the phone in my life and the complainant must be asked to prove if he has ever spoken to me on the phone as he claims we are friends. I used to work for one Mr. Manjeet Singh who had some dealings with the complainant. Once I was in need of some money and a bank official was visiting Mr. Manjeet Singh and I gave my blank signed cheque with some documents to him to process a loan for me from the bank. I do not know how the complainant has come in possession of that cheque. My employer Mr. Manjeet Singh has since run away. He was neighbours with the complainant." 5. Seeing the plea of defence, the trial was converted from summary to a summons case and upon request of the Accused person, Complainant was recalled for facing cross-examination. LEGAL POSITION 13. Section 138 NI Act has certain ingredients which must all be cumulatively satisfied to constitute the offence of CC NI Act No.3634/2021 DLWT020208662021 Digitally signed VAIBHAV by VAIBHAV PRATAP SINGH PRATAP Date:

case_name: Joginder Pal Salhotra vs Mangat Singh
text: Digitally signed VAIBHAV by VAIBHAV PRATAP SINGH PRATAP Date: SINGH 2024.08.07 16:28:05 +0530 cheque bouncing. The cheque must be drawn by a person on an account being maintained by him for payment of money, which is presented within its validity period and it should be issued for discharging a legally enforceable debt or liability. If the cheque is returned dishonoured by the bank due to insufficient funds, or a host of other reasons by virtue of Laxmi Dyechem v. State of Gujarat (2012) 133 SCC 375, a demand for the said amount of money must be made in writing within one month of the receipt of information of dishonour. If the payment is not made within 15 days of receiving the notice of demand, the complaint may be made within a month thereafter. 14. The law on the issue is well settled and does not need elaborate discussion. Paragraph 7 of the judgment of the Hon'ble Supreme Court of India in Tedhi Singh v. Narayan Dass Mahant , 2022 INSC 266 sums up the position best and is quoted hereunder: "7. It is true that this is a case under Section 138 of the Negotiable Instruments Act. Section 139 of the N.I. Act provides that Court shall presume that the holder of a cheque received the cheque of the nature referred to in Section 138 for the discharge, in whole or in part, of any debt or other liability. This presumption, however, is expressly made subject to the position being proved to the contrary. In other words, it is open to the accused to establish that there is no consideration received. It is in the context of this provision that the theory of 'probable defence' has grown. In an earlier judgment, in fact, which has also been adverted to in Basalingappa, this Court notes that Section 139 of the N.I. Act is an example of reverse onus [see (2010) 11 SCC 441). It is also true that this Court has found that the accused is not expected to discharge an unduly high standard of proof. It is accordingly that the principle has developed that all which the accused needs to establish is a probable defence. As to whether a probable defence has been established is a matter to be decided on CC NI Act No.3634/2021 DLWT020208662021 Digitally signed VAIBHAV by VAIBHAV PRATAP SINGH PRATAP Date:

case_name: Joginder Pal Salhotra vs Mangat Singh
text: Digitally signed VAIBHAV by VAIBHAV PRATAP SINGH PRATAP Date: SINGH 2024.08.07 16:28:10 +0530 the facts of each case on the conspectus of evidence and circumstances that exist." POINT FOR DETERMINATION 15.The foundational facts that lead to raising of the above- mentioned presumptions are the execution and issuance of the impugned cheque. The cheque in question has been dishonoured for the reason "Kindly Contact Drawer/ Drawee Bank and please present again". The Accused Person in the present case has admitted the cheque in question and his signatures on it and thus this Court is now bound to presume that the impugned negotiable instrument passed for consideration and was issued to discharge a legally enforceable debt. In addition to this, the return memo is also admitted. The ingredient of non-payment within fifteen days after receipt of statutory notice is not disputed. Thus, four of the ingredients of Section 138 NI Act, as noted above, are undisputed. Dispute has been raised only as to the existence of a legally enforceable debt, which is presumed. To rebut the presumption of the legally enforceable debt, he has chosen to cross-examine the Complainant to poke holes in his case. 16.Other ingredients not being in dispute, the only point for determination is whether or not a probable defence has been raised by the Accused Person by which the existence of a legally enforceable debt or liability has been made doubtful from the standard of preponderance of Digitally signed VAIBHAV by VAIBHAV PRATAP PRATAP SINGH Date: 2024.08.07 SINGH 16:28:14 +0530 CC NI Act No.3634/2021 DLWT020208662021 probabilities. However, first, this Court must deal with the issue of maintainability of the Complaint. REASONING AND FINDINGS DEFECTIVE OATH IN THE PSE AFFIDAVIT

case_name: Joginder Pal Salhotra vs Mangat Singh
text: REASONING AND FINDINGS DEFECTIVE OATH IN THE PSE AFFIDAVIT 17.Accused Person has taken exception to the maintainability of the present complaint as CW-1, in his cross- examination, has stated that the PSE-affidavit was never sworn before the Oath Commissioner and was executed by him at his house, where only he and his lawyer were present. It is argued that as per the rules of the Hon'ble Delhi High Court, the Oath Commissioner shall only attest affidavits in the presence of the deponents and failure to do so would lead to termination of the Oath Commisioner. Reliance, additionally, is placed on the judgment of the Hon'ble Delhi High Court in Salazar Luis Anthony Marques v. Mohd. Haroon Japanwala , 2014 SCC OnLine Del 1903. In response, Complainant has argued that a bare perusal of the PSE Affidavit would show that it bears the stamp and signature of the Oath Commissioner and this plea does not hold any water. 18.This Court has given anxious consideration to this issue as, if accepted, it would non-suit the Complainant at the outset. In Salazar Luis (supra), the Hon'ble Court was dealing with proceedings under the Delhi Rent Control Act ("DRC"), Section 25B(4) of which requires a tenant filing CC NI Act No.3634/2021 DLWT020208662021 Digitally signed VAIBHAV by VAIBHAV PRATAP SINGH PRATAP Date: SINGH 2024.08.07 16:28:20 +0530 a leave to defend to support it by a supporting affidavit. Holding that an affidavit without oath is not an affidavit at all, the Court rejected the leave to defend. 19.This precedent may not squarely apply to the case at hand for more than one reasons. First, it was dealing with civil law and especially, a special statute which was interpreted keeping its purpose, procedure, and provisions in mind. Second, in the precedent cited, there was no oath at all while in the case at hand, the affidavit contains oath but it has been admitted by the deponent that no oath commissioner was present to administer it. This by itself may not be sufficient to declare the oath invalid. Third, Section 7 of the Oaths Act, 1969 itself postulates that no omission to take any oath and no irregularity whatever in the administration of any oath shall invalidate any proceeding or render inadmissible any evidence whatever.

case_name: Joginder Pal Salhotra vs Mangat Singh
text: 19.This precedent may not squarely apply to the case at hand for more than one reasons. First, it was dealing with civil law and especially, a special statute which was interpreted keeping its purpose, procedure, and provisions in mind. Second, in the precedent cited, there was no oath at all while in the case at hand, the affidavit contains oath but it has been admitted by the deponent that no oath commissioner was present to administer it. This by itself may not be sufficient to declare the oath invalid. Third, Section 7 of the Oaths Act, 1969 itself postulates that no omission to take any oath and no irregularity whatever in the administration of any oath shall invalidate any proceeding or render inadmissible any evidence whatever. 20.Finally, in the case at hand, the Complainant/CW-1 stepped into the witness box post-summoning and adopted the pre- summoning evidence affidavit, after being administered oath by this Court. Thus, in the considered opinion of this Court, this cannot be called a case of 'no evidence' as even if it is assumed that the evidence at the per-summoning stage was defective for want of proper oath, the evidence at the post-summoning stage being considered at this stage was given after oath was duly administered to the Complainant/CW-1 by this Court. Digitally signed VAIBHAV by VAIBHAV PRATAP SINGH PRATAP Date: SINGH 2024.08.07 16:28:24 +0530 CC NI Act No.3634/2021 DLWT020208662021 REASON FOR DISHONOUR 21.The reason for dishonour of the impugned cheque Ex.CW- 1/1 by the return memo Ex.CW-/2 is for the reason "Kindly Contact Drawer/ Drawee Bank and please present again". Nowhere in the Complaint or in the PSE Affidavit has it been averred that the Complainant re-presented the impugned cheque at all, let alone did so after contacting the drawer or the drawee bank. The Complainant, though mentioned bank witnesses it its list of witnesses, did not examine the drawee bank to bring on record the actual reason for the cheque being dishonoured. 22.Whether this reason for dishonour would constitute an offence under Section 138 NI Act has engaged the attention of multiple constitutional courts, a few of which may appositely be referred. 23.The Hon'ble Madras High Court in its order/judgment dated 24.07.2019 in Crl.OP. 10406/2019 titled " Sathiya Murthi v. Kesava Narayan " held as follows:

case_name: Joginder Pal Salhotra vs Mangat Singh
text: 22.Whether this reason for dishonour would constitute an offence under Section 138 NI Act has engaged the attention of multiple constitutional courts, a few of which may appositely be referred. 23.The Hon'ble Madras High Court in its order/judgment dated 24.07.2019 in Crl.OP. 10406/2019 titled " Sathiya Murthi v. Kesava Narayan " held as follows: "5. The only ground that has been raised by the learned counsel for the petitioner is that, the cheque has been returned by the bank by requesting the respondent to contact the Drawer-Drawee Bank and present it again. The respondent without complying with the requisite has proceeded to file the complaint for an offence under Section 138 of the Negotiable Instrument Act. It is true that the Hon'ble Supreme Court of India has held that, for whatever reasons a cheque is returned, a complaint can be maintained under Section 138 of the Negotiable Instruments Act and if CC NI Act No.3634/2021 DLWT020208662021 VAIBHAV Digitally by VAIBHAV signed PRATAP Date: 2024.08.07 PRATAP SINGH SINGH 16:28:29 +0530 such a purposeful interpretation is not given to the provision, it will defeat the beneficial legislation which wants to penalize the persons who fail to honour a cheque, after issuing the same. 6. However, in the facts of the present case, the concerned bank has not returned the cheque on the ground of defect. It has been returned by directing the respondent to contact a concerned bank and present it again. In short, this endorsement cannot be held to be dishonour of cheque. In the considered view of this Court, the respondent without complying with the requirements of the bank, ought not to have proceeded to file a complaint under Section 138 of the Negotiable Instruments Act. The Court below went wrong in taking cognizance of the complaint." (emphasis supplied) 24.The Hon'ble Telangana High Court in its order/judgment dated 13.12.2022 in Crl.P. 3492/2022 titled " D.S. Shridhar v. P. John " held as follows: "3. Under Section 138 of the Negotiable Instruments Act, prosecution can be launched if the cheque is returned unpaid for the reason of 'insufficient funds' or 'it exceeds arrangement to be paid'. The Hon'ble Supreme Court held that apart from two reasons, if the reasons are 'account closed' and 'payment stopped' by the drawer' are also liable under Section 138 of the Negotiable Instruments Act.

case_name: Joginder Pal Salhotra vs Mangat Singh
text: 24.The Hon'ble Telangana High Court in its order/judgment dated 13.12.2022 in Crl.P. 3492/2022 titled " D.S. Shridhar v. P. John " held as follows: "3. Under Section 138 of the Negotiable Instruments Act, prosecution can be launched if the cheque is returned unpaid for the reason of 'insufficient funds' or 'it exceeds arrangement to be paid'. The Hon'ble Supreme Court held that apart from two reasons, if the reasons are 'account closed' and 'payment stopped' by the drawer' are also liable under Section 138 of the Negotiable Instruments Act. 4. As seen from the cheque, it was printed in the year 2008. The Bank has returned the cheque by specifically mentioning reason of 'kindly contact drawer/drawee bank and please present again' which is in the handwriting of the banker stating that drawer or drawee bank has to be contacted and then present the cheque. 5. As stated in the complaint, the question of returning the cheque for 'insufficient funds' does not arise. As seen from the cheque return memo, the reason of 'funds insufficient' is number 6. The Bank has returned twice for the reason 19 stating 'kindly contact drawer/drawee bank and please present again' which was handwritten. When the reason for returning the cheque does not conform to the twin requirements mentioned under Section 138 of the Negotiable Instruments Act or if the account is closed and payment stopped by drawer as stated by the Hon'ble Supreme Court, CC NI Act No.3634/2021 DLWT020208662021 Digitally signed VAIBHAV by VAIBHAV PRATAP SINGH PRATAP Date: SINGH 2024.08.07 16:28:33 +0530 the reason mentioned by the Banker to contact the drawer or drawee bank and present again will not amount to a reason for which the prosecution under Section 138 of the Negotiable Instruments Act can be launched. 6. If the complainant is aggrieved and in the facts of the case if an offence of cheating as defined under Section 415 of IPC is made out, complainant is at liberty to prosecute accordingly. Since there are no ingredients to launch prosecution under Section 138 of the Negotiable Instruments Act, the proceedings against the petitioner are liable to be quashed." (emphasis supplied)

case_name: Joginder Pal Salhotra vs Mangat Singh
text: 6. If the complainant is aggrieved and in the facts of the case if an offence of cheating as defined under Section 415 of IPC is made out, complainant is at liberty to prosecute accordingly. Since there are no ingredients to launch prosecution under Section 138 of the Negotiable Instruments Act, the proceedings against the petitioner are liable to be quashed." (emphasis supplied) 25.The case at hand is covered by the above-noted precedents. The return memo Ex.CW-1/2 in the present matter bearing the reason "Kindly Contact Drawer/ Drawee Bank and please present again" and the Complainant herein directly filing the present complaint without having done so does not bring the dishonour within the ambit of Section 138 NI Act. The Accused Person is entitled to acquittal on this ground alone. However, since evidence has been led and arguments have been advanced on merits and the evidence of the case, this Court proposes to deal with the same. LEGALLY ENFORCEABLE DEBT 26.The defence of the Accused Person is that he does not know the Complainant at all and does not know how the Complainant came to be in possession of his cheque. He has never taken a loan from the Complainant. The Complainant was known to his erstwhile employer Mr. Mangat Singh. One day, when a banker was visiting Mr. Mangat Singh, the Accused Person herein sought a loan for CC NI Act No.3634/2021 DLWT020208662021 VAIBHAV Digitally by VAIBHAV signed PRATAP Date: 2024.08.07 PRATAP SINGH SINGH 16:28:37 +0530 which the impugned cheque was given to Mr. Mangat Singh. The loan was never processed and the cheque, as it turned out, has been misused in the present proceedings. 27.During final arguments, Accused Person also argued that the Complainant is a money-lender and the Accused Person has since learnt that the Complainant is predatory in his practices, including the abuse of NI Act jurisdiction and the Accused Person also learnt of someone who died due to the pressure tactics employed by the Complainant while recovering the loan. 28.After going through the evidence, it can be seen that Complainant/CW-1 in his cross-examination has put up a case which has left this Court non-plussed. Let alone inspiring confidence, it has led this Court to believe that the Accused Person's defence is not only probable but correct.
