Let alone inspiring confidence, it has led this Court to believe that the Accused Person's defence is not only probable but correct. 29.Complainant/CW-1 deposed that he knows the Accused Person for the last 15 or so years but has never had a meal or even a cup of tea with him. He had never even visited the house of the Accused Person before allegedly granting the loan in question. 30.Complainant/CW-1 stated that he visited the house of the Accused in his professional capacity as a contractor 2-3 times for 5-10 minutes each, during which time, the mother of the Accused Person became like Complainant's own CC NI Act No.3634/2021 DLWT020208662021 Digitally signed VAIBHAV by VAIBHAV PRATAP SINGH PRATAP Date: SINGH 2024.08.07 16:28:41 +0530 mother prompting him to give the unsecured loan in question to the Accused Person without any paperwork whatsoever. This is when Complainant/CW-1 specifically stated that he is not friends with the Accused Person. 31.Needless to say, it was suggested to him that he has never been to the house of the Accused Person nor ever met his mother let alone becoming family with her. Curiously, despite claiming that the loan in question was given for the medical treatment of the mother of the Accused Person, Complainant/CW-1 did not even know what ailed the mother of the Accused Person, whom he professes to love like his own mother. 32.Not surprisingly, Complainant/CW-1 deposed that the loan in question was given to the Accused Person in cash at the house of the Complainant while nobody else was present and without executing any kind of paperwork at all.

Curiously, despite claiming that the loan in question was given for the medical treatment of the mother of the Accused Person, Complainant/CW-1 did not even know what ailed the mother of the Accused Person, whom he professes to love like his own mother. 32.Not surprisingly, Complainant/CW-1 deposed that the loan in question was given to the Accused Person in cash at the house of the Complainant while nobody else was present and without executing any kind of paperwork at all. 33.Even more ludicrously, Complainant/CW-1 deposed that out of the loan in question of INR 2,70,000/-, Complainant/CW-1 only had INR 1,30,000/- available with him and had to himself borrow the balance amount from a friend of his whose name he does not even know. Since the mystery friend runs a shop called Sunny Jewelers, he is known to the Complainant as Sunny Jeweler. In other words, Complainant/CW-1 took a loan from a person whose name he does not know to further loan it to the Accused Person, with whom he was never friends. CC NI Act No.3634/2021 DLWT020208662021 Digitally signed VAIBHAV by VAIBHAV PRATAP SINGH PRATAP Date: SINGH 2024.08.07 16:28:47 +0530 34.CW-1 further stated in his cross-examination that he has not been working for gain since 2020, prior to which he was a property dealer and a 'financier'. He stated that he was only a financial advisor though he was not registered with SEBI or any other authority. 35.Complainant/CW-1 further deposed that he had financial dealings with Mr. Manjeet Singh which caused him financial losses. It was suggested to him that due to the financial dealings between him and Mr. Manjeet Singh going south, Mr. Manjeet Singh gave to the Complainant the cheque of the Accused Person to do with it as he pleased, which suggestion was denied. 36.Complainant/CW-1 further deposed that he is an income tax payee but has not disclosed the loan in question in his concerned ITR. He further deposed that he generally fills ITRs of Rs. 4 lakhs to Rs. 5 lakhs. CC NI Act No.3634/2021 DLWT020208662021 Digitally signed VAIBHAV by VAIBHAV PRATAP SINGH PRATAP Date: SINGH 2024.08.07 16:28:52 +0530 37.Accused Person has argued that the Complainant has not shown any source of funds to grant the loan in question and that there are material contradictions in the testimony of CW-1 in chief and in cross-examination.

case_name: Joginder Pal Salhotra vs Mangat Singh
text: SINGH 2024.08.07 16:28:52 +0530 37.Accused Person has argued that the Complainant has not shown any source of funds to grant the loan in question and that there are material contradictions in the testimony of CW-1 in chief and in cross-examination. 38.Complainant has, despite being challenged, not given any proof of income or source of funds. It takes a fatal hit at his credibility that he does not even know the name of his friend from whom he claims to have borrowed the money to further loan it to the Accused Person. His own disclosed income being around INR 4-5 lacs a year, it is highly improbable that he loaned more than half his yearly income to the Accused Person by taking a loan himself without so much as taking a security cheque. As per Complainant/CW-1 himself, the cheque in question was given later for repayment and was not given as security at the time of allegedly advancing the loan in question. Complainant himself admittedly did not have the financial capacity to grant the loan in question. 39.In a case of cheque bouncing for a friendly loan, the Complainant is not expected to show in the first instance that he had the funds available with him to give the loan in question. However, if his paying capacity is challenged by the Accused, he must show that he had the financial capacity to give the loan in question. [See Tedhi Singh (supra).]. Digitally signed VAIBHAV by VAIBHAV PRATAP SINGH PRATAP Date: SINGH 2024.08.07 16:28:56 +0530 CC NI Act No.3634/2021 DLWT020208662021 40.Non-disclosure of the income or the loan in the ITR to reflect payment capacity is a ground amounting to rebutting the statutory presumptions under NI Act . [See Rajaram v. Maruthachalam , 2023 SCC OnLine SC 48].

case_name: Joginder Pal Salhotra vs Mangat Singh
text: 40.Non-disclosure of the income or the loan in the ITR to reflect payment capacity is a ground amounting to rebutting the statutory presumptions under NI Act . [See Rajaram v. Maruthachalam , 2023 SCC OnLine SC 48]. 41.The reason stated for the big-heartedness is that the mother of the Accused Person became like the mother of the Complainant in only two-three visits to his house for 5-10 minutes each as a 'contractor'. This Court wonders what professional work exactly a Contractor does at a potential site in 5-10 minutes leaving enough time to form blood- relations with family members of the client. Despite this Shravan Kumar-like posturing, it was outside of the knowledge of the Complainant/CW-1 as to what medical condition the mother of the Accused Person was suffering with for which financial assistance was being given. This Court is of the opinion that this does not even formally need to be rejected as it is so far beyond the scope of plausibility, and yet, this Court must and does give a finding that this story is beyond unbelievable and is hereby discarded. 42.The acrobatics the Complainant/CW-1 has shown in his cross-examination when questioned about his professional ongoings perhaps gives insight into this case. It is writ- large that the Complainant is involved in questionable financial dealings, one of which, as per the Accused Person, is misusing 138 NI Act proceedings to seek unlawful gain. This complaint appears to be an outrageous CC NI Act No.3634/2021 DLWT020208662021 Digitally signed VAIBHAV by VAIBHAV PRATAP SINGH PRATAP Date: SINGH 2024.08.07 16:29:00 +0530 attempt to initiate criminal proceedings against a person unknown to the Complainant by merely obtaining his cheque leaf, understandably from Mr. Manjeet Singh, with whom the Complainant himself admitted during final arguments he is no longer in touch. 43.The phrase "preponderance of probabilities" in the case of Charles R. Cooper v. F.W. Slade, (1857-59) 6 HLC 746 was held to mean "more probable and rational view of the case" and this definition has stood the test of time, having been quoted with approval several times by Indian Courts, including as recently as in 2022 in Ramanand v. State of U.P. , 2022 SCC OnLine SC 1396 para 100. CONCLUSION
